content.assessment: This is the most structurally ambitious proposal and correspondingly faces the highest coalition-resistance profile, since it directly confronts both the ideological commitment of the governing coalition to stratification and the concrete veto power of gimnázium directors who have demonstrated access to top leadership. The proposal's own risk analysis acknowledges phased implementation as a hedge against Polish-style reversal shock, which is a politically sound design feature, but the underlying reversal risk is structural rather than merely implementational, since the tracks were themselves post-1990 policy layers that can be reframed as either 'status quo' or 'temporary policy' depending on which coalition holds power.
opportunities: Because the tracks are themselves a 1990s policy addition rather than an ancient institution, reformers have a legitimate framing resource to characterize the comprehensive default as the 'true' status quo

Bundling with anti-sorting catchment rules could build a broader coalition addressing multiple selection points simultaneously, potentially increasing perceived comprehensiveness and legitimacy of the reform package
strengths: Phased multi-cohort transition design directly incorporates a documented feasibility lesson (Poland's 2017 single-year reversal shock) rather than ignoring implementation-sequencing risk

Targets the age-10/12 selection point identified as producing stronger selection effects than in three of four comparator countries, addressing a well-evidenced inequality mechanism rather than a peripheral one
threats: Constitutional free-choice guarantees may be invoked to challenge catchment/anti-sorting bundling, creating a judicial reversal vector independent of legislative politics

Church/foundation/private schools outside Klebelsberg jurisdiction may resist inclusion or be exempted, preserving a selective channel that undermines the reform's systemic legitimacy and invites the criticism that reform only reshuffles rather than eliminates stratification

Finland's own PISA decline and emergent informal sorting practices show that even successfully enacted structural delay does not guarantee durable political or educational payoff, weakening the long-term legitimacy case for reformers
weaknesses: Directly threatens the core interest of gimnázium directors, an organized group with demonstrated direct access to the Prime Minister-designate, making this proposal the most exposed to elite veto-player resistance among all five

Confronts an explicit ideological commitment of the 2010-2026 governing coalition to stratification as policy, not merely an administrative default, raising the political cost of enactment substantially
